author: @MichaelDobbins
text:
I love the trade-off for this design of simplicity, ease of building and good power production, etc. Following this theme you, in an earlier version, and several here suggested to add an extra opposing magnet plate to increase magnetic strength. 

I would like to extend this by adding an additional serpentine coil and winding plate to add an extra phase. A true 2 phase (not split phase) version would smooth ripple and and increase efficiency and power, close to 3 phase but without the complexity of 3 phase. 
To build: 2 winding plates and 2 coils. Assemble a coil in each winding plate, but the plates together open facing open, so no need for covers. Rotate each from the other halfway way between coil lands i.e. 1/36th circumstance, so that the sin waves are 90° off from each other, ex. when one wave is max (+/-) the other is 0. Attach one end of each coil together to make a common N. The open ends become L1 and L2. Connect N, L1, L2 to a 3 phase full bridge rectifier to get DC. 
Use 2 magnet plates, one in each side of the pair of winding plates.  

Everyone focuses on 3 phase for good reason. 3 phase is the most efficient for power vs wire metal content over long distances. If you are generating for direct 3 phase connection, the most important design and implementation aspect is matching the 3 phase parameters. 

The brilliance of this single phase generator for variable input generators is that none of the 3 phase parameters matter to charge a battery. What matters is to get as much variable power input converted to DC and into a battery as efficiently as possible. This single phase generator does that with a simple easy to implement design. The proposed 2 phase is an extension leveraging the underlying simplicity using all the same parts and construction techniques. It reduces DC ripple (maybe not that important) and increasing effective power(voltage).  Here are some calculations for 1, 2 and 3 phase ripple&power to compare:
1 phase: DC abs(sin(x)) = 0 - 1, range 1, ave 0.5,  voltage/coil 0.5
2 phase: DC abs(sin(x)) + abs(sin(90+x)) = 1 - 1.414,  range 0.414, ave 1.207, voltage/coil 0.6035
3 phase: DC abs(sin(x))+abs (sin (120+x))+abs (sin(240+x)) = 1.161-2.628, range 1.467, ave 1.8945, voltage/coil 0.6315

author: @DavidSmith-fx5xp
text:
Hi Robert, I'm new to all this generator making, I'm in the early process of installing a home battery system (Inverter and 2 batteries, total = 19Kw).
However, instead of solar, I want to make some of your 3D printed wind generators to generate our electricity. I estimate I need 3-4 small generators totalling 2-3 Kw.  My question is "What combination of wire gauge, the number of turns, best coil shape, magnet shape, magnet strength, should I use to produce a reliable yet powerful small generator?"
Is there a chart somewhere where I can view the above information and get the correct combination of parts to create a 1KWh wind generator?
Your advice would be very appreciated.
Thank you.

author: @robertoguerra5375
text: When you reject the idea of adding steel cores, you are following the coreless motor philosophy: trade torque (or current capacity for generators), in exchange for weight reduction.

author: @MAGATRON-DESTROY
text: If you add steel rotors to hold the magnets and have two opposing rotors you will get much more power due to highly focused flux and there have actually been studies done on this. Purely plastic, copper and magnets will give you less power. It would be easy to incorporate steel rotors in a 3D printed model, just make the part that holds the magnets without a back and the magnets would hold the steel and then glue it altogether

author: @Jwfcreates
text: Great videos thank you. I’ve printed the wind turbine and also built the serpentine flux generator. However I’m new to all this, and can’t test for current… not getting any readings from my multi meter… should I be testing for AC or DC current… should I be able to test the continuity? I have clipped the test cables to each end of the copper coil.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: yeah test for continuity - it will tell you if you broke the coil on winding or placing - it happens to me so the first test i always do is continuity - after that output is always ac from this kind of generator - for dc you will need to rectify it. After that you need to put a load on it - no load no amps as amps only ever push a load the best load is  resistor with the same value of resistance as your coil

author: @Zer0PointUnlimited
text:
It would be interesting to see what kind of DC voltage you would get if you make it a homopolar generator....instead of alternating magnets, put them all north on one side, and all south on the other. Typically the winding is made flat (pancake coil), but this setup is a nice form-factor to test. 
P.S. a ferrous "keeper" on the back-side would most certainly increase the magnetic field seen by the coil.

replies.author: @MAGATRON-DESTROY
replies.text:
For serpentine coils make the bends match the number of magnets and in a N S N S pattern. 
For regular coils use a 3/4 coil/magnet ratio when doing 3 phase. I think it's a 1/1 ratio for a single phase machine but I don't make them. I'd ask Roberto about that last one

text: Rob, that is a belt sizer.  When your car or lawnmower breaks a belt, you hold it together, and place it around the two pieces, and slide them apart, until you reach the size of your belt.  Then, you read the length on the tool, and you know what belt to buy.

replies.author: @kadmow
replies.text:
If winding lots of turns is too tedius "one could" wind multiples simultaneously as you asked - and then join them together (series - multiple tapped) to make a single coil (need to Identify winding / flow direction of each coil accurately - connect tip to tail), also if the single coil can't cope with the current (amount of charge flowing through the wire instantaneously heats up the wire leading to thermal fatigue) parallelling increases the current capacity - only necessary if approaching the thermal limits of the wire.  

Remember Ohms law I = V/R   and Electrical Power = IV, you can never get out more than you put in.  (Mechanical power = Work / Time, or Force x Velocity).  (The old BLV Sine(theta) - shows that; length, speed, "angle" and field strength, all impact the outcome "equally" - length is the easiest to design into the system (or vary) - thickness of wire impacts "survivability.

replies.author: @Zane.1376
replies.text:
@kadmow that is what I was stating about starting with the three thin wires but the thick wire added only for adding amps while keeping the voltage.
Thinner wire higher voltage, thicker wire higher amperage. Combine the two

replies.author: @kadmow
replies.text: @Zane.1376  - no, more wire length in the same field = more volts (at the same velocity - moving it faster is the other variable, or get stringer magnets). Thicker wire is only really needed if heating effects (Amps, ie charge flowing  through wire) are fatiguing the wire (or increasing resistive loses), or catching the insulation on fire. (reread my other post, it is in there)..  cheers, have 2 laughs on me.

replies.author: @Zane.1376
replies.text: @kadmow well think of it as water flowing through a pipe. The bigger the pipe the more water can flow at one moment in time. With a thicker wire you have increased amperage but, less voltage. So combine the two. If worried about the heat smother the wires in thermal paste and have an air flow around the coil. This is too low of a voltage to worry about that though.

author: @deadpoet4
text: That coil former is great!  And...  If both side slide up and down the rail, you can attach the center of the rail to an axel and handle, the run your wire spool off another axel.  Spin the former to wind your coil.  Add a Geneva drive counter, and spin away!  2000 turns?  No problem!  Add a motor and a circuit to count the turns / shut  off the motor after a set number;  get a coil with a push of a button!

replies.author: @russellzauner
replies.text: Yeah, I kind of want to make a super lightweight axial flux generator for my bike - it doesn't seem to have much pushback so I can pull energy while I'm pedaling because my mid drive doesn't have regen.  I still need to figure out magnetic brakes without the weight of a whole motor.  It would be cool if I could make just a pure set of regen brakes but I lack the experience and skill.  Once I make a bunch of stuff I'll be able to figure out how to put them together to get what I need.  I think it's a great product because you can keep your freewheel but you get the benefit of brakes you can use at high speeds without setting them on fire - I live in a basically mountainous area (North Oregon Coast Range/Columbia River Gorge) that can have elevation changes of hundreds of feet within blocks in some areas and up to 1500ft elevation.

The other thing to consider is the size of wire thicker will produce more amps.  if I was thinking about making one of these for my self I would come up with what I would like for amps so I could decide on the wire size because you can also change your voltage with speed.  I hope that makes sense

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: no mate - wire thickness doesn't effect amps produced - it enables higher amps to be handled - more force input produces more amps

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: Volts = BLV sine theta, it's Faraday's law

The last while its been charging coils on small engines where space is limited voltage needs to be enough to charge a battery without cooking it and still run electric clutches winch and lift motors and so on all through a single diode.  so my excuse is I replied from my shop computer currently surrounded by thick wire big diodes and charging coils I should  be be putting together and not commenting on video's lol

author: @jasonmorello1374
text: If you had a plate carriage on the opposite side of the coil, where you have a pair sized plate, this will have a similar amplification as if you had another set of matching magnets of close to it, and yes I know Rob mentioned this. I bring it up because of way to bring up the power with complexity if you feel it warrants, such as two coils, offset, and two plate carriages, where thee coils mount to housing(so you don't need brushes), and the carriages and plates mount to the axle. This will only double the width, but probably triple the power intersection. Small chance it could push on the current load the wire can handle, but I doubt that without much higher dollar magnets. I also think this power up  design, of one set of axial magnets, two coils, and two plate carriages, would have a similar boost for the thunderbolt design. The question really is would these improve the conversion efficiency as significantly as it seems they could? Iron core coils/electromagnets are about 10x stronger than air core, so I think it could. It may have more base force to start moving tho.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I am sorry mate - but 'magnetic amplification'? - a plate directs the flux path it doesn't amplify

replies.author: @ravenmad9225
replies.text: I think by 'amplify' he just means improve efficiency and therefore output.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: oh ok - well if that is what he means he is right - but for axial flux designs the improvement is pretty minor - check out this paper for a discussion on this https://ietresearch.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/full/10.1049/elp2.12218 - rotors made of epoxy were shown to be pretty much as good

replies.author: @jasonmorello1374
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering Amplification is wrong in some sense. What it is a straightening of flux across the coil zone, which makes for a better crossing angle of the device. I guess better combed or collated would be more accurate.

replies.author: @jasonmorello1374
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering I looked into that paper, and figure 13 shares a lot with the pattern I was thinking of, a little hard to see if it is documented out on any of the charts tho.

author: @johnbelcher7955
text: I don't really understand the electrical side of this, but is it possible to put a coil on either side of the magnets and produce voltage from either side?

replies.author: @mikestrain4747
replies.text: from what I understand you could arrange the coil and magnet any way you like although you might need to modify the 3D print. I don't know the exact numbers but the amount of power output is related to the strength of the magnetic field so it could be you get less power from 2 coil. on one magnet disk then from 1 coil with the 2 magnet disk.  I have also seen interesting versions using multiple magnet and coil disks
